title: GPDE Workshop - Ancient type II solutions of the curve shortening flow and the Ricci flow on surfaces
speaker: Panagiota Daskalopoulos

proof_stages.label: Ancient convex curve shortening flow in pressure variables
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the CSF classification problem for ancient closed convex embedded curves, recalls curvature and angle-coordinate pressure equations, defines Type I/II behavior, and states the contracting-circle versus Angenent-oval alternatives.

proof_stages.label: CSF backward steady state from Harnack and Lyapunov control
proof_stages.summary: The Aronson-Bénilan/Harnack inequality gives monotonicity of the pressure and existence of a backward limit, while a bounded functional forces the time derivative to vanish weakly so that the limit satisfies the steady-state equation away from its zero set.

proof_stages.label: CSF zero-set classification and monotone rigidity
proof_stages.summary: A Poincaré-type interval argument controls the zeros of the limiting pressure, and a second monotone quantity for α=pθ forces equality and hence the exact contracting-circle or Angenent-oval form, with a closing discussion of what is known beyond convexity.

proof_stages.label: Surface Ricci flow in conformal pressure variables
proof_stages.summary: The Ricci-flow problem is set up for ancient compact surface metrics by writing g=u gS², converting the flow to logarithmic fast diffusion and pressure equations, defining Type I/II behavior, and stating the classification into contracting spheres and Rosenau solutions.

proof_stages.label: Ricci backward convergence and two-point zero-set bound
proof_stages.summary: Using positivity of scalar curvature, C1,α estimates, and a nonstandard bounded Lyapunov functional, the proof obtains a weak steady backward limit and then shows its zero set has at most two points via curvature concentration, local estimates, blow-up to the cigar soliton, and quantization.

proof_stages.label: Conformal normalization of the Ricci backward limit
proof_stages.summary: Once at most two zeros are known, a conformal change moves them to the poles and Mercator projection to the cylinder reduces the steady equation to Δ log w=0, forcing the normalized limit to be c cos²ψ, with c≥0.

proof_stages.label: Zero-limit branch gives contracting spheres
proof_stages.summary: For c=0, Hamilton’s isoperimetric-ratio evolution inequality produces curves whose length estimates contradict uniform blow-up of the conformal factor unless the ancient solution is the shrinking round sphere.

proof_stages.label: Positive-limit branch gives Rosenau solutions
proof_stages.summary: For c>0, radial ancient solutions are classified on the cylinder by applying a barrier-assisted maximum principle to q=wxx−4w, and then stereographic projection plus monotonicity of the angular derivative shows all such solutions are radial, hence Rosenau.

examples.name: Complicated embedded curve becoming convex under curve shortening flow
examples.note: The speaker recalls the Grayson-type phenomenon that even a complicated embedded plane curve becomes convex before extinction, illustrating the special two-dimensional behavior behind the convex CSF setup rather than generating the classification proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker cites the known theorem that even a complicated embedded nonconvex curve becomes strictly convex before singularity, without analyzing a particular curve

passes.reason: A nonconvex embedded plane curve is an ordinary input for Grayson's theorem, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.

examples.name: Grim-Reaper asymptotic picture for the Angenent oval
examples.note: In response to a question, the Angenent oval is described at backward infinity as resembling two Grim Reapers or parallel lines at finite separation, making the Type II CSF model visually concrete without being used in the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the Grim-Reaper picture is used only as an asymptotic description of the Angenent oval at minus infinity, with no concrete computation or construction carried out on it

passes.reason: The Grim-Reaper/Angenent-oval asymptotic is a standard ancient/translator model, not a sporadic or maximally symmetric object, and is used to describe the backward limit.
The grim-reaper pair is used descriptively as the backward asymptotic model for the Angenent oval, not as a refuting example or sporadic object.
The Grim Reaper is a self-similar translating soliton with strong space-time regularity, used here as the Angenent oval's asymptotic model rather than as a counterexample.
the Grim Reaper is a translating soliton/limiting asymptotic model for the Angenent oval rather than a counterexample

examples.name: Possible nonconvex spiral ancient curves
examples.note: The claimed but nonrigorous spiral-type ancient curves illustrate why dropping convexity is delicate, since nonconvex flows are no longer encoded by the same porous-medium pressure equation.
examples.engagement_reason: possible nonconvex spiral ancient curves are discussed as a claimed/nonrigorous possibility and open issue, but no specific spiral is constructed or checked

passes.reason: The proposed nonconvex spiral ancient curves are ordinary hypothetical spiral-type solutions but are raised as possible enemy cases to convexity or classification without convexity.

examples.name: Higher-dimensional Yamabe and mean-curvature-flow analogues
examples.note: The final open-problem discussion mentions Rosenau-type Yamabe-flow solutions and expected mean-curvature-flow ancient ovals to show how the two-dimensional classifications fit into a broader geometric-flow landscape.
examples.engagement_reason: higher-dimensional Yamabe and mean-curvature-flow analogues are raised as open-problem analogies, with no detailed derivation or worked example
